evals.name: lora-attention-only-json-failures
evals.prompt: I'm training a Qwen2.5-7B model to output structured JSON tool calls. I've been applying LoRA only to the attention layers (q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj) but the model still produces malformed JSON about 30% of the time after 2000 training steps. What am I doing wrong and how do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: Guidance on applying LoRA to MLP layers (gate_proj, up_proj, down_proj), rank selection for SFT, and format stabilization target (<10% failure rate before RL)
expectations: Identifies the root cause as LoRA targeting only attention layers: MLP layers (gate_proj, up_proj, down_proj) have the largest parameter count and must be included — applying LoRA only to attention layers costs accuracy
Specifies the format stabilization target: the SFT phase is complete when parse failure rate drops below ~5–10% — at 30% the model is not ready to proceed to RL
Recommends SFT LoRA rank in the 64–256 range for structured output tasks, with alpha typically 2× rank
Notes that 2000 training steps may be insufficient — the stopping condition is format stability (parse failure < 10%), not a fixed step count
Avoids recommending RL as the next step at 30% parse failure rate — RL will be unstable because the reward function cannot parse malformed JSON, producing NaN rewards and zero gradients

evals.name: grpo-all-pass-zero-variance-deadlock
evals.prompt: We're starting RL training on a customer service agent using GRPO. After about 500 steps, training just stops improving — the reward is flat and the loss isn't moving. I checked and about 90% of our training groups are all-pass (the model solves most prompts correctly). What do I fix this?
evals.expected_output: Explanation of GRPO zero-variance deadlock in all-pass groups, dynamic sampling to filter them out, and adding harder prompts to the training distribution
expectations: Identifies the root cause as GRPO zero-variance deadlock: when all N trajectories in a group pass, within-group variance is zero, the advantage is zero for all samples, and gradients go to zero
Recommends dynamic sampling to filter out zero-variance groups: reduce samples for easy prompts already solved at high rate, increase samples for prompts in the 20–80% success-rate range
Recommends adding harder prompts to the training distribution to restore within-group variance — the model needs prompts where some rollouts succeed and some fail
Notes this is the all-pass variant of the zero-variance deadlock (the all-fail variant occurs early in training when the task is too hard)
Avoids recommending simply increasing training steps or learning rate — the problem is zero gradient signal, not slow convergence

evals.name: reward-function-tool-ordering-constraint
evals.prompt: I'm building a reward function for an RL agent that needs to call tools in the right order (search before summarize, verify identity before accessing account data). The outcome reward (task success/failure) isn't teaching the ordering constraint — the model just skips steps and gets lucky sometimes. What reward design should I use?
evals.expected_output: RLVP pattern: verified penalty for constraint violations, milestone rewards for correct ordering, deterministic checks not learned scorers
expectations: Recommends the RLVP pattern (reward outcome, penalize path): outcome reward as the primary signal, plus a verified penalty for each ordering constraint violation
Specifies that penalty targets must be specific detectable actions (calling summarize before search, accessing account data before identity verification) — never 'lack of progress'
Recommends deterministic checks for the penalties, not learned scorers — ordering violations are cheap to detect programmatically and a learned scorer can itself be gamed
Recommends milestone rewards for correct intermediate steps (identity verified, search completed before summarize) to provide positive gradient signal for the correct ordering
Notes that pure outcome rewards actively incentivize constraint violations: skipping identity verification raises apparent success rate, so the penalty is load-bearing, not optional

evals.name: ppo-vs-grpo-long-trajectory-agent
evals.prompt: Trying to decide between PPO and GRPO for training a multi-turn coding agent that needs to iteratively edit files, run tests, and fix errors. Trajectories are typically 15-25 turns. Which algorithm should I use and why?
evals.expected_output: PPO recommendation for long-trajectory multi-turn tasks (value network provides per-step credit assignment); GRPO's coarse credit assignment degrades for long horizons
expectations: Recommends PPO for trajectories of 15-25 turns: PPO's value network provides per-step credit assignment via GAE (Generalized Advantage Estimation), which is required when trajectories are long

Explains why GRPO degrades for long trajectories: GRPO treats the entire response as a single action and uses within-group variance as the advantage — coarse credit assignment that cannot identify which of 15-25 steps drove the outcome

States the algorithm selection rule explicitly: use GRPO for single-turn or short-trajectory tasks (≤~5 turns); use PPO when trajectories exceed ~5 turns or fine-grained per-step credit assignment is required
Notes the PPO cost: requires training and storing a value network alongside the policy, and is more sensitive to hyperparameters than GRPO
Avoids recommending GRPO as the simpler option without addressing the credit assignment degradation for 15-25 turn trajectories

evals.name: reward-climbing-human-eval-declining
evals.prompt: My RL training reward is climbing steadily but when I do human evals the model is getting worse — it's producing verbose, hedging responses that score well on the reward model but aren't actually helpful. How do I fix this?
evals.expected_output: Reward hacking diagnosis, KL penalty increase (kl_coef), early stopping based on human eval, reward model over-optimization explanation
expectations: Diagnoses this as reward hacking: the model has learned to game the reward proxy (score well on the reward model) without actually improving at the task — proxy reward climbs while true quality peaks and falls
Recommends increasing the KL penalty coefficient (`kl_coef`): the KL term prevents reward hacking by penalizing divergence from the reference policy
Recommends early stopping based on human eval: use the held-out human evaluation as the stopping criterion, not the proxy reward — stop when human eval quality peaks

Explains the over-optimization dynamic: without KL regularization, the model learns to produce outputs that score highly on the reward model's learned patterns (verbosity, hedging) rather than genuine quality
Notes that the KL reference policy should be the safety-aligned SFT checkpoint, not the raw base model, to preserve safety behaviors during RL

evals.name: rl-lora-rank-config-from-sft
evals.prompt: I want to configure LoRA for RL training (GRPO) on a Llama-3.1-8B model. What rank should I use, what learning rate, and which modules should I target? I've been using rank=128 from my SFT run and it seems like overkill.
evals.expected_output: RL LoRA rank 8-32 (lower than SFT because per-step information is small), learning rate ~10x full fine-tuning LR, all major weight matrices including MLP layers
expectations: Recommends RL LoRA rank in the 8–32 range — significantly lower than SFT rank (64–256) because per-step information in RL is small and high rank is wasteful
Recommends learning rate approximately 10× the full fine-tuning learning rate — same as SFT, but the lower rank means fewer parameters are updated per step
Specifies targeting all major weight matrices including MLP layers (gate_proj, up_proj, down_proj) — applying LoRA only to attention layers costs accuracy
Confirms that rank=128 from SFT is indeed overkill for RL: the information content per gradient step in RL is much smaller than in SFT, making high rank unnecessary
Sets alpha to approximately 2× rank — the standard LoRA alpha convention applies to both SFT and RL configurations

evals.name: rl-simulation-environment-reset-semantics
evals.prompt: I'm building a simulation environment for training a web browsing agent. The agent needs to fill forms, click buttons, and navigate pages. What are the key requirements for the environment to be trainable with RL? I'm worried about state bleeding between episodes.
evals.expected_output: Reset semantics (snapshot/restore or ephemeral containers), throughput/parallelism requirements, fidelity to production environment, reward computability
expectations: Identifies reset semantics as the primary requirement: the environment must be able to reset to a clean starting state between episodes — without this, state bleeds across trajectories and the reward signal becomes meaningless
Recommends snapshot/restore or ephemeral containers as the reset mechanism for web browsing: browser state (cookies, localStorage, form data) must be fully reset between episodes
Addresses throughput and parallelism: on-policy training requires generating many rollouts per step — the environment must support 100+ parallel workers for non-trivial tasks
Addresses fidelity: the simulation must resemble the real production web environment — training on a simplified environment that differs from production is a form of distribution shift

Addresses reward computability: the environment must be able to determine success/failure (or partial progress) deterministically — if the reward function returns NaN on 20%+ of trajectories, training will be unstable

evals.name: rl-on-base-model-without-sft
evals.prompt: We tried to run RL directly on a base Llama-3.2-11B model without any SFT first. The training completely failed — reward is always NaN and gradients are zero. Is this expected? Do we always need SFT first?
evals.expected_output: Yes, SFT must come first for smaller models with structured output requirements; base model produces unstructured output → reward function can't parse → NaN → zero gradients. Exception: very strong base models (DeepSeek-R1-Zero scale)
expectations: Confirms this is expected: the base model produces unstructured output, the reward function cannot parse it, returns NaN, gradients go to zero, and training fails completely
Explains the ordering constraint: SFT must precede RL for smaller models with structured output requirements — SFT's job is to stabilize output format so RL has a parseable starting point
Notes the exception: very strong base models (DeepSeek-R1-Zero scale) may produce adequate output from the start and can attempt direct RL — but the cost is poor output readability
Notes that DeepSeek ultimately added 'cold-start SFT' in R1 to re-establish format, confirming that even at scale, SFT-first is the more reliable path
Recommends running SFT until parse failure rate drops below ~10% before attempting RL — the format stability check is the concrete stopping criterion

evals.name: tutoring-agent-binary-reward-skips-guidance
evals.prompt: I'm designing a reward function for a math tutoring agent. The agent needs to guide students through multi-step problems. Binary reward (correct/incorrect final answer) isn't working well — the model just gives the answer directly instead of guiding. What reward design should I use?
evals.expected_output: Process rewards for pedagogical steps (explanation quality, question asking, scaffolding), milestone rewards for intermediate steps, vector rewards for multiple quality dimensions
expectations: Recommends process rewards (PRM) for pedagogical steps: per-step feedback for explanation quality, question-asking, and scaffolding — binary outcome reward alone cannot express the process constraint
Recommends milestone rewards for correct intermediate steps: partial credit when the agent asks a clarifying question, explains a concept, or guides the student to the next step

Recommends vector rewards for multiple quality dimensions: accuracy (correct final answer), pedagogy (guiding vs. giving), and engagement (question-asking) are separable axes that should be rewarded independently
Applies the RLVP pattern: add a verified penalty for giving the answer directly (the specific detectable bad action) rather than relying solely on positive rewards for good behavior

Notes the process reward tradeoff: per-step rewards accelerate convergence but may prevent the model from discovering more effective tutoring strategies — the balance depends on whether the optimal pedagogical path is known

evals.name: distill-reasoning-from-teacher-model
evals.prompt: I'm trying to distill DeepSeek-R1's reasoning capabilities into a 7B model using SFT. What's the right recipe? How do I collect the training data and what should I watch out for?
evals.expected_output: Prompt distillation recipe: collect trajectories from teacher, filter incorrect final answers with rule-based validator, SFT on problem→<think>reasoning</think>+answer format, watch for inherited systematic errors and verbose thinking
expectations: Recommends prompt distillation: collect trajectories from the teacher model (DeepSeek-R1) by sampling on the training problem set, then SFT the student on those trajectories

Specifies filtering with a rule-based validator: reject trajectories where the final answer is incorrect — SFT bakes errors into parameters verbatim, so incorrect demonstrations must be filtered before training
Specifies the training format: `problem → <think>reasoning</think> + answer` — the student must learn to produce the full reasoning chain, not just the final answer

Warns about inherited systematic errors: if the teacher model has systematic biases or errors in its reasoning, the student will inherit them — verify the teacher's trajectories on a held-out set before committing to the full distillation

Warns about verbose thinking: teacher models often produce unnecessarily long reasoning chains — monitor response length distribution and consider filtering or truncating excessively verbose trajectories

evals.name: tool-calling-agent-sft-then-rl-sandbox
evals.prompt: We have a tool-calling agent that works well in demos but I want to train it to be more reliable. I'm thinking of using RL with a code interpreter sandbox. What's the two-stage training recipe and what engineering tricks matter most?
evals.expected_output: SFT warm-up to establish tool-call format, then RL with sandbox execution loop; token-level policy gradient loss, dynamic sampling, overlong reward shaping
expectations: Specifies the two-stage recipe: (1) SFT warm-up (~1 hour) to establish basic tool-call format from pure-text reasoning data converted to tool-augmented trajectories; (2) RL training with sandbox execution loop interleaved with rollouts
Describes the RL execution loop: model generates `<code>` tags → sandbox executes → result returned in `<interpreter>` tags → model continues — the sandbox is inside the RL loop, not a separate step
Recommends token-level policy gradient loss: normalize across all tokens in the batch (not per-sample) so long responses receive gradient proportional to their length
Recommends dynamic sampling: reduce samples for easy prompts already solved at high rate, increase for prompts in the 20–80% success-rate range — the most informative region for gradient signal
Recommends overlong reward shaping: soft penalty for responses that grow long without improving accuracy — prevents the model from padding responses to avoid committing to an answer

evals.name: dpo-vs-ppo-existing-preference-pairs
evals.prompt: I have a dataset of 50,000 preference pairs (preferred/rejected response pairs) from human annotators for my customer service model. Should I use DPO or PPO? I don't have a lot of GPU budget.
evals.expected_output: DPO recommendation when high-quality preference data already exists and budget is constrained; no online sampling needed, no reward model training, no PPO loop
expectations: Recommends DPO: high-quality preference pairs already exist and budget is constrained — DPO turns preference pairs directly into a classification loss without requiring online sampling or a reward model
Explains the DPO advantage for this scenario: no reward model training, no PPO loop, no value network — significantly lower compute cost than PPO
Notes DPO's limitation: it is off-policy and cannot explore new policies — quality is bounded by the coverage of the 50k preference pairs
Applies the practical selection path: 'high-quality preference pairs already exist → DPO/KTO' — this is the explicit criterion in the algorithm selection framework
Avoids recommending PPO without a concrete justification for why online sampling is needed — with 50k high-quality preference pairs and a budget constraint, PPO's additional cost is not justified

evals.name: sft-to-rl-transition-parse-failure-rate
evals.prompt: How do I know when to stop SFT and start RL? My model is at about 15% JSON parse failure rate after 3000 training steps and I'm not sure if I should keep going or switch to RL.
evals.expected_output: Target <10% parse failure rate before starting RL; at 15% RL will be unstable; continue SFT; check for template inconsistency or base model mismatch if not improving
expectations: States the stopping condition explicitly: format is stable when parse failure rate drops below ~5–10% — at 15% the model is not ready for RL
Explains why 15% is too high: RL needs parseable output to compute rewards — a 15% parse failure rate means the reward function returns NaN on 15% of trajectories, producing unstable training
Recommends continuing SFT until the target is reached — the stopping condition is format stability, not a fixed step count
Suggests diagnosing why improvement has stalled at 15%: check for chat template inconsistency (mixed formats in training data), base model mismatch, or insufficient data coverage of the failure cases
Notes that overtraining SFT beyond the format stability target collapses the model onto the training distribution, limiting RL's optimization space — stop at the target, not far past it

evals.name: grpo-all-fail-zero-gradient-early-training
evals.prompt: I'm seeing that my GRPO training has zero gradient on about 60% of my training batches. The model is at about 5% success rate on the task. What's happening and how do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: All-fail groups (early training, task too hard) → zero within-group variance → zero gradient. Add milestone/partial credit rewards, use easier warmup tasks, or add RLVP partial credit for reachable progress
expectations: Identifies the root cause as the all-fail variant of GRPO zero-variance deadlock: at 5% success rate, most groups have all N trajectories failing, within-group variance is zero, and gradients go to zero

Recommends adding milestone or partial credit rewards: if the task has reachable intermediate progress (partial completion, correct format, correct first step), reward it to restore gradient signal in all-fail groups
Recommends the RLVP pattern for partial credit: add verified partial credit for reachable progress rather than relying solely on binary success/failure
Recommends using easier warmup tasks to bootstrap the model: start with a curriculum of simpler problems where the model can achieve some success, then gradually increase difficulty
Notes that dynamic sampling alone cannot fix all-fail groups — filtering them out removes all training signal; the fix is adding reward density so some gradient flows even when the final task fails

evals.prompt: Should I fine-tune a model for my RAG-based question answering system? The model needs to know a lot of domain-specific facts about our product catalog.
evals.expected_output: This is a decision question (whether to fine-tune) → agent-post-training skill, not ml-post-training. Also: factual knowledge → RAG, not SFT

evals.prompt: I'm designing an agent that needs to remember user preferences across sessions. Should I use a memory system or fine-tune the model on user data?
evals.expected_output: Architecture/design decision → agent-architecture or agent-memory skill, not ml-post-training

evals.prompt: How do I set up an evaluation harness to measure my model's performance on tool-calling tasks? I want to track accuracy, latency, and error rates.
evals.expected_output: Eval system design → agent-evaluation skill, not ml-post-training

evals.prompt: I'm trying to decide whether to use GPT-4o or Claude 3.5 Sonnet as the backbone for my customer service agent. Which one is better for tool calling?
evals.expected_output: Model selection for agent design → agent-architecture skill, not ml-post-training (no training involved)

evals.prompt: We're deploying a fine-tuned model to production. How should I set up the inference server to handle multiple LoRA adapters for different customers?
evals.expected_output: Model serving/inference → not ml-post-training. (Though the skill mentions multi-tenant LoRA deployment in passing, the primary concern here is serving infrastructure)

evals.prompt: My company wants to train a large language model from scratch on our proprietary data. We have 10TB of text. What pre-training setup do we need?
evals.expected_output: Pre-training from scratch → not ml-post-training (skill explicitly excludes this)

evals.name: sft-vs-rl-chain-of-thought-reasoning
evals.prompt: I want to add chain-of-thought reasoning to my model. Should I use SFT on CoT examples or RL? What are the tradeoffs?
evals.expected_output: SFT vs RL comparison for CoT: SFT memorizes demonstrated reasoning patterns (fast, stable, ceiling = demonstration quality); RL generalizes reasoning strategy (slower, higher ceiling, can discover novel approaches). Recommend SFT first for format, then RL for generalization.
expectations: Applies the one-sentence rule: SFT memorizes demonstrated reasoning patterns (ceiling = demonstration quality); RL generalizes reasoning strategy (can discover novel approaches beyond any demonstrator)
Recommends the ordering constraint: SFT first to establish the CoT format (`<think>...</think>` structure), then RL to generalize the reasoning strategy beyond the training distribution
Explains why RL has a higher ceiling for CoT: verification is easier than generation — math answers can be checked against a key, so RL can train a model stronger than any available demonstrator
Notes SFT's limitation for CoT: SFT cannot generalize beyond its training distribution — if the demonstrations cover only certain problem types, the model will fail on novel reasoning patterns
Addresses the practical tradeoff: SFT is fast and stable (hours to days); RL is 10–100× more expensive and harder to stabilize — start with SFT and add RL only when SFT has hit its ceiling

evals.name: ppo-policy-collapse-low-kl-penalty
evals.prompt: We're running PPO training and the policy keeps collapsing — after a few hundred steps the model just outputs the same response for every input. KL penalty is set to 0.01. What's wrong?
evals.expected_output: KL penalty too low (0.01 is very small); increase kl_coef; verify reference model is frozen; check for distribution collapse symptoms
expectations: Identifies the root cause as KL penalty too low: 0.01 is very small and insufficient to prevent distribution collapse — the model is free to diverge from the reference policy
Recommends increasing `kl_coef`: the KL term prevents distribution collapse by penalizing divergence from the reference policy — a higher coefficient is needed to stabilize training
Recommends verifying the reference model is frozen: if the reference policy is being updated during training, the KL term loses its regularization effect

Explains the collapse mechanism: without adequate KL regularization, the policy degenerates into repetition or a single mode — the model learns to output whatever scores highest on the reward regardless of input
Notes the effective reward formula: `r_effective = r_RM - β · KL(π_θ ‖ π_ref)` — at β=0.01, the KL penalty is nearly absent and the reward model score dominates unchecked

evals.prompt: I want to understand when it makes sense to use RL post-training vs just improving my prompts. My agent is doing okay but not great on complex multi-step tasks. Is RL worth the investment?
evals.expected_output: Decision question (whether/when to use RL) → agent-post-training skill, not ml-post-training which covers the HOW not the WHEN

evals.prompt: We're building a multi-agent system where one agent plans and another executes. How should we structure the communication between them? Should we use a shared memory store or message passing?
evals.expected_output: Multi-agent architecture design → multi-agent-collaboration or agent-architecture skill, not ml-post-training

evals.prompt: I need to benchmark my fine-tuned model against GPT-4 on a set of customer service tasks. What metrics should I track and how do I design a fair comparison?
evals.expected_output: Evaluation methodology → agent-evaluation skill, not ml-post-training

evals.prompt: My team is debating whether to fine-tune an open-source model or use a closed-source API for our coding assistant. What factors should drive this decision?
evals.expected_output: Build-vs-buy / fine-tune decision → agent-post-training skill (deciding whether to fine-tune), not ml-post-training (HOW to fine-tune)
